summary: O'Neil recounts her traumatic year as a graduate student at Princeton's math department, describing an intensely male-dominated, antisocial environment hostile to women. She develops a theory that Princeton has optimized itself to produce 'geniuses' modeled on Einstein, which by definition excludes women and minorities. She then critiques Susan Patton's advice for women to get plastic surgery and husband-hunt in college, arguing that while Patton correctly identifies the shift to a 'free market' mating system, her strategies are both degrading and doomed to fail. The essay connects Princeton's worship of male success with Patton's call for women to objectify themselves.
key_points: Princeton's math department culture is described as extremely male-dominated, antisocial, and frozen in time around Einstein-era ideals of genius worship
The town's advice to women seeking connection was to join a church or have children as 'something to do' - revealing women's roles as supporting characters to potential male geniuses
Susan Patton's advice to get plastic surgery and husband-hunt represents the logical extreme of a culture that values women only for their ability to serve successful men
Modern dating has shifted from social contract to 'free market' dynamics where men assert masculinity through the sexual attractiveness of their partners to other men
Patton's strategies are self-defeating because withholding sex and pushing for early marriage signals desperation in the current system
money_quotes: There are two kinds of people in the world: people who hate Princeton, New Jersey, and people who are über successful white men.
I did suspiciously have my boobs with me. I must be kitchen staff.
If it helps, I think I eventually see every female graduate student at Princeton.
Would-be geniuses are worshipped in weird ways, and anyone who is not themselves a genius candidate has to tailor themselves to those who are.
And since by definition geniuses are not women – and nor are minority men – we know what their roles turn out to be.
Women, at least white women, are seen as useful in as much as they can have man-children who may grow up to be geniuses.
It's really a homo-erotic, all-male issue: be something that other men will be jealous of.
key_insight: Elite academic institutions like Princeton optimize for a narrow definition of genius that systematically excludes women, creating a culture where women's only valued role is to support or attract successful men.
